sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, UP, RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 63, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 32 of about 123 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the maze structure more clearly. Looking at the ascii:
- Rows 30-34: GGGGGGGGGG (gray wall) at cols 20-29, then c (charcoal) at cols 30-35, then G at col 36
- Rows 35-39: cccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG - there's a gap (c) at cols 9-18 and 30-39
- Rows 40-41: GccccccccGGGGGOOOOOcccccGGGGGG - the target (O) is at cols 25-29
- Rows 42-44: GccccccccGGGGGbbbbbcccccGGGGGG - the blue part (b) is at cols 25-29

- Cols 1-9: ccccccccc (9 chars)

This matches the segmentation: orange at cols 34-38.
Now let me look at the maze structure:
- Rows 30-34: GGGGGGGGGG at cols 20-29 (wall), then c at cols 30-35, then G at col 36
  Wait, row 30: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
  Cols 20-29: cccccccccc (10 chars, all c)
  Cols 30-39: GGGGGGGGGG (10 chars, all G)
  Cols 40-44: ccccc (5 chars, all c)
  Col 45: G
So rows 30-34 have a wall (G) at cols 30-39. This blocks the target from moving UP from rows 35-36.
Row 35: `ccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
Cols 20-29: cccccccccc (10 chars, all c)
Cols 30-39: GGGGGGGGGG (10 chars, all G)
Cols 40-49: cccccccccG (9 c's and 1 G)
Wait, this also has a wall at cols 30-39. But the target is at rows 35-36, cols 29-33 or 34-38. If there's a wall at cols 30-39, how can the target be at cols 34-38?
Hmm, let me re-examine. The ascii shows G for gray walls and c for charcoal background. The target is O (orange) and b (blue).
Row 35: `ccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
If the target is at rows 35-36, cols 29-33, then the ascii should show O at those positions. But the ascii shows G at cols 30-39.
Wait, maybe the ascii is from a different frame. Let me check the current target position.
Actually, the current target is at rows 40-41, cols 34-38 (orange). The ascii for row 40 shows:
`GccccccccGGGGGOOOOOcccccGGGGGG`
Cols 20: G

Cols 30-33: GGGG

- Left: GGGG (cols 30-33)
- Right: ccccc (cols 39-43) then GGGGGG (cols 44-49)
- Above: need to check row 39
- Below: need to check row 42
Row 39: `GccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
Cols 30-39: GGGGGGGGGG (wall)
So above the target (at rows 40-41), there's a wall at row 39, cols 30-39. But the target can move UP to rows 35-36. How?
Wait, the target is 4 rows tall (orange at rows 40-41, blue at rows 42-44). When I move UP, the target moves up 5 rows. So from rows 40-41, UP moves to rows 35-36. But there's a wall at row 39, cols 30-39.
Hmm, maybe the wall at row 39 doesn't block the target because the target is only 2 rows tall (orange) and the blue part is separate. Or maybe the wall has a gap that I'm not seeing.
Actually, looking at row 39 more carefully:
`GccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
Cols 20: G
Cols 21-29: ccccccccc
Cols 30-39: GGGGGGGGGG

Wait, maybe I'm misreading the ascii. Let me look at the full row 39:
`GccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
Actually, I printed `line[20:50]`, which is cols 20-49. The full row might be longer. Let me check the full row.
Actually, the grid is 64x64. Let me print the full row 39.
But wait, the key insight is: the target CAN move UP from rows 40-41 to rows 35-36. So there must be a path. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.
Row 35-36: `ccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
This shows a wall at cols 30-39. But the target is at cols 29-33 or 34-38 in these rows. If there's a wall at cols 30-39, the target can't be at cols 34-38.
Unless... the ascii representation is different from what I think. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the issue is that the ascii shows the CURRENT frame, where the target is at rows 40-41, cols 34-38. When the target moves to rows 35-36, the ascii would show O at those positions, replacing the G's.
So the wall at rows 35-36, cols 30-39 is actually a path that the target can occupy. The G's in the ascii represent the wall when the target is not there, but when the target moves there, it replaces the G's with O's and b's.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. If it's a wall, the target can't move there. But the target CAN move to rows 35-36, cols 34-38.
Let me re-examine the ascii. Maybe the G's at rows 35-36, cols 30-39 are not walls but part of the path.
Actually, looking at the ascii again:
Row 35: `ccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGccccccccccG`
Row 40: `GccccccccGGGGGOOOOOcccccGGGGGG`
The difference is that row 40 has OOOOO at cols 34-38, while row 35 has GGGGGGGGGG at cols 30-39. This suggests that rows 35-36 are walls (G) and rows 40-41 are paths (c) with the target (O).
But the target can move UP from rows 40-41 to rows 35-36. So rows 35-36 must be paths, not walls.
I think the ascii is misleading me. Let me look at the segmentation instead.
Actually, I realize the ascii shows the current state. When the target is at rows 40-41, the ascii shows O at those positions. When the target moves to rows 35-36, the ascii would show O at rows 35-36 and the previous positions would show c or G.
So the G's at rows 35-36, cols 30-39 are the background when the target is not there. When the target moves there, the O's replace the G's.
This means the path at rows 35-36 is actually a gray (G) corridor, and the target can move through it. The G's are not walls but the floor color.
OK so the maze structure is:
- The target moves through gray (G) corridors.
- The charcoal (c) areas are walls or background.
- The target is confined to a 2x2 grid of positions within the gray corridors.
Given this, the target can't escape the confined area because the charcoal (c) areas block it.
Now, what's the goal? The target is confined and can't reach the blue cross or yellow markers. So the goal must be something else.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to move the target to a specific position within the confined area, and that position triggers something. Or maybe the goal is to move the target in a specific pattern.
Actually, I just realized something. In level 1, the goal was to move the target to the blue cross. In level 2, maybe the goal is the same, but the target is confined. So maybe there's a way to "teleport" or "warp" the target to the blue cross position.
Or maybe the goal is to move the target to a position where it overlaps with the blue cross's column or row, which triggers a level completion.
Let me check the blue cross position again. The blue cross is at rows 41-43, cols 15-17. The target is at rows 40-41 (orange), cols 34-38 and rows 42-44 (blue), cols 34-38.
The target's blue part is at rows 42-44, which overlaps with the blue cross's rows 41-43. But the columns don't overlap (34-38 vs 15-17).
Hmm, maybe the goal is to move the target to a position where the blue part is at the same row as the blue cross. The target's blue part is currently at rows 42-44, which overlaps with the blue cross's rows 41-43. So maybe the goal is already achieved?
But the level hasn't completed. So this doesn't seem right.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the target to a position where it "collects" or "activates" the yellow markers. But the target can't reach the yellow markers.
Wait, maybe the yellow markers are not physical objects but rather indicators of where the target should go. For example, maybe the yellow marker at rows 51-52, cols 40-41 indicates that the target should be at that position.
But the target can't reach that position. So maybe the goal is to move the target as close as possible to the yellow marker.
The target is at rows 40-41, cols 34-38. The yellow marker is at rows 51-52, cols 40-41. The closest the target can get is rows 40-41, cols 34-38 (which is 11 rows above and 2-7 columns to the left of the yellow marker).
Hmm, this doesn't seem right either.
Let me try yet another approach. Maybe the goal is to move the target to a position where it aligns with the player (white cross). The player is at rows 46-47, cols 51-52. The target is at rows 40-41, cols 34-38. These are far apart.
Or maybe the goal is to move the target to a position where it "points" toward the player or the blue cross.
I'm running out of ideas. Let me try to look at the level from a higher level again.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the movement. The target moves 5 cells per action. The confined area is a 2x2 grid with positions 5 cells apart. This suggests the maze is designed with 5-cell corridors.
Let me check if there are any other corridors the target can access. Maybe I missed a path.
From the ascii:
- Rows 30-34: GGGGGGGGGG at cols 30-39 (corridor)
- Rows 35-39: GGGGGGGGGG at cols 30-39 (corridor)
- Rows 40-44: GGGGG at cols 30-33, OOOOO/bbbbb at cols 34-38, ccccc at cols 39-43, GGGGGG at cols 44-49
So the corridor at cols 30-39 extends from rows 30 to 39. The target is at rows 40-44, cols 34-38, which is below the corridor.
Wait, but the target can move UP to rows 35-36, which is within the corridor. So the target can access the corridor at rows 30-39, cols 30-39.
Let me check if the target can move further UP within the corridor. From rows 35-36, UP would move to rows 30-31. Let me try that.
Actually, I already tested this earlier and UP from rows 35-36 was blocked. So the target can't move further UP within the corridor.
Hmm, but the corridor extends from rows 30 to 39. Why can't the target move to rows 30-31?
Maybe the corridor has walls at certain positions. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.

Row 31: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
Row 32: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
Row 33: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`

The corridor at cols 30-39 is present in rows 30-35. But the target can only reach rows 35-36, not rows 30-34.
Wait, maybe the corridor is blocked by something at rows 30-34. Let me check if there are any obstacles.
Actually, looking at the ascii, rows 30-34 have GGGGGGGGGG at cols 30-39, which is the same as rows 35-39. So the corridor is continuous from rows 30 to 39.
But the target can only reach rows 35-36. This suggests there's a barrier or wall at rows 30-34 that blocks the target.
Wait, but the ascii shows G (gray) at rows 30-34, cols 30-39, which is the same color as the corridor. So maybe the G's are not walls but the floor color, and the target can move through them.
But the target can't move UP from rows 35-36 to rows 30-31. So there must be something blocking the target.
Let me re-examine the movement. When I press UP from rows 40-41, the target moves to rows 35-36 (5 cells up). When I press UP from rows 35-36, the target would move to rows 30-31 (5 cells up). But this is blocked.
So there's a barrier at rows 30-34 that prevents the target from moving further UP. But the ascii shows G (gray) at those positions, which is the same as the corridor.
Maybe the barrier is not visible in the ascii but is detected by the game logic. Or maybe the barrier is at a different position.
Actually, I think the issue is that the target is 4 rows tall (orange at 2 rows, blue at 2-3 rows). When the target moves UP from rows 35-36, the orange part would move to rows 30-31, and the blue part would move to rows 32-34. But maybe the blue part collides with something at rows 32-34.
Let me check the ascii for rows 32-34:
Row 32: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
Row 33: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
Row 34: `ccccccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGcccccG`
These rows have GGGGGGGGGG at cols 30-39, which is the corridor. So the blue part shouldn't collide with anything.
Hmm, I'm confused. Let me try a different approach: let me look at the full ascii to understand the maze structure better.
Actually, let me just try moving the target to rows 30-31 by pressing UP from rows 35-36 and see what happens. Maybe my earlier test was wrong.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Target at rows 40-41, cols 34-38
# Let me try to explore further UP
# First go UP to rows 35-36
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"UP: {n['boundary']}")

# Try UP again to see if it goes further
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"UP again: {n['boundary']}, no_impact={result.get('no_impact')}")

# Let me also check the full ascii around rows 25-40, cols 25-45 to see the maze
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print("\nMaze area (rows 25-45, cols 25-50):")
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if 25 <= i <= 45 and i < len(lines):
        print(f"{i:2d}: {line[25:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Target at rows 40-41, cols 34-38
# Let me try to explore further UP
# First go UP to rows 35-36
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"UP: {n['boundary']}")

# Try UP again to see if it goes further
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"UP again: {n['boundary']}, no_impact={result.get('no_impact')}")

# Let me also check the full ascii around rows 25-40, cols 25-45 to see the maze
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print("\nMaze area (rows 25-45, cols 25-50):")
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if 25 <= i <= 45 and i < len(lines):
        print(f"{i:2d}: {line[25:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
